R E S E A R C H A R T I C L E
Parental herbivory shapes offspring performance across
genotypes in an invasive clonal plant
Y.-F. Zhao 1 , Q.-Y. Fu1
, H.-C. Chang1
, B.-C. Dong 1,2 & F.-H. Yu3
1 School of Ecology and Nature Conservation, Beijing Forestry University, Beijing, China
2 The Key Laboratory of Ecological Protection in the Yellow River Basin of National Forestry and Grassland Administration, Beijing Forestry University, Beijing,
China
3 Institute of Wetland Ecology and Clone Ecology/Zhejiang Provincial Key Laboratory of Plant Evolutionary Ecology and Conservation, Taizhou University,
Taizhou, Zhejiang, China
Keywords
Genotypic variation; herbivore pressures;
Hydrocotyle verticillata; plant invasion; trans-
generational effect.
Correspondence
B.-C. Dong, School of Ecology and Nature
Conservation, Beijing Forestry University,
Beijing 100083, China.
E-mail: bcdong@bjfu.edu.cn
Ying-Fei Zhao and Qiu-Yue Fu contributed
equally to this work.
Editor
C. Lara-Romero
Received: 14 December 2025;
Accepted: 22 January 2026
doi:10.1111/plb.70193
ABSTRACT
• Parental environmental effects play a crucial role in clonal plants, potentially enabling
clonal offspring to pre-adapt to environmental changes. However, few studies have
examined whether such parental effects differ between genotypes and whether they
enhance both growth and defence.
• We conducted a two-generation herbivory experiment using six genotypes of the inva-
sive clonal plant Hydrocotyle verticillata and the native generalist herbivore Spodoptera
litura in China. We tested how parental herbivory (PH) effects inﬂuence offspring sur-
vival, growth, and herbivory defence, and whether these parental effects vary among
genotypes.
• Herbivory signiﬁcantly reduced leaf mass and leaf number of parental plants, while
increasing its speciﬁc leaf area. PH enhanced offspring survival by 21% and tended to
increase offspring leaf mass. However, clonal offspring from herbivory-treated parental
plants suffered equally severe defoliation (>95%) as control offspring. While most
parental effects were consistent across genotypes, root mass and speciﬁc leaf area
showed genotype-dependent responses. Notably, genotype G4 exhibited markedly ele-
vated SLA following PH despite having the lowest offspring survival.
• Our results reveal an asymmetric transgenerational strategy in invasive H. verticillata,
that is, PH enhances offspring growth and survival without conferring herbivory
defence. This decoupling of growth enhancement from defence induction represents
an evolutionary trade-off that may facilitate rapid colonization but leaves invasive
populations vulnerable to herbivore pressure. Consequently, while this transgenera-
tional plasticity facilitates range expansion and population establishment in
herbivore-free habitats, it may render invasive populations susceptible to regulation by
specialist or generalist herbivores during secondary contact.
INTRODUCTION
Plant invasions are a primary driver of global environmental
change, causing native biodiversity loss and ecosystem degrada-
tion (Pyˇsek et al. 2020; IPBES 2023). The invasion success of
alien plants is often explained by the Enemy Release Hypothesis
(ERH), which posits that invasive plants gain competitive advan-
tages by escaping their co-evolved specialist herbivores, allowing
resource reallocation from defence to growth and reproduction
(Keane 2002; Liu & Stiling 2006; Xu et al. 2021). This evolution-
ary realignment may leave invasive populations particularly vul-
nerable to novel herbivore encounters, but paradoxically, many
invasive species thrive despite herbivory from generalist herbi-
vores in their introduced ranges (Huang et al. 2010; Schaffner
et al. 2011). Understanding how invasive plants maintain advan-
tages under herbivore pressure without their ancestral defensive
mechanisms remains a critical gap in invasion ecology.
Transgenerational plasticity may provide a rapid adaptive
mechanism for invasive plants facing novel herbivore
communities (Agrawal 2001; Sobral et al. 2021; Fu et al. 2023).
Parental environmental effects, whereby parental stress expo-
sure modiﬁes offspring phenotypes, can enable populations to
respond to environmental challenges faster than genetic adap-
tation alone (Engqvist & Reinhold 2016; Yin et al. 2019). In
clonal plants, these effects may be particularly pronounced as
vegetative reproduction maintains direct physiological connec-
tions between generations (Dong et al. 2018a; Luo et al. 2022).
Such transgenerational priming could theoretically compensate
for lost defensive capabilities by enhancing offspring growth
vigor or inducing anticipatory defences. However, the effective-
ness of transgenerational plasticity in conferring herbivory
defence remains largely untested in invasive contexts, particu-
larly when invasive populations have undergone enemy release.
Herbivory represents one of the most prevalent and ecologi-
cally signiﬁcant stresses for plants, directly constraining growth
through tissue loss and resource depletion (Schoonhoven
et al. 2005). Recent evidence suggests herbivory effects can per-
sist across generations, with parental herbivory (PH) either
Plant Biology
© 2026 Wiley-VCH GmbH. Published by John Wiley & Sons Ltd.
1
Plant Biology ISSN 1435-8603
compromising offspring through reduced provisioning or
enhancing offspring performance through adaptive priming
(Herman & Sultan 2011; Holeski et al. 2012). In invasive popu-
lations that have evolved reduced constitutive defences, the
direction and magnitude of these transgenerational effects
become critical: enhanced growth provisioning might sustain
rapid population expansion, while defensive priming could
compensate for lost defence traits (Fenesi et al. 2014; Sobral
et al. 2021). Yet, whether invasive plants can maintain both
growth enhancement and defensive capabilities through trans-
generational mechanisms remains unknown.
Genetic variation fundamentally shapes both plastic
responses and their transgenerational transmission (Lavergne
& Molofsky 2007; Forsman 2014). Different genotypes may
vary substantially in their capacity for transgenerational plastic-
ity, with some lineages exhibiting strong parental effects while
others show minimal responsiveness (Li et al. 2016). This geno-
typic variation in transgenerational responses could maintain
population-level resilience even under limited genetic diversity
– a common characteristic of invasive populations. However,
most studies examining PH effects focus on single genotypes
(Dong et al. 2017, 2019; Verhoeven et al. 2018; Kaﬂe &
Wurst 2019), obscuring the potential for genotype-speciﬁc
strategies that collectively enhance invasion success (Agra-
wal 2001; Colicchio 2017).
Understanding transgenerational effects in invasive clonal
plants is particularly important because these species often
establish large populations from limited genetic material, mak-
ing parental conditioning potentially crucial for invasive popu-
lation success in novel environments. Therefore, we studied the
invasive clonal plant Hydrocotyle verticillata Thunb. and its
interactions with the generalist herbivore Spodoptera litura
(Fabricius, 1775) in China to investigate transgenerational
responses to herbivory across multiple genotypes. This system
also provides an ideal model as H. verticillata exhibits substan-
tial genotypic variation despite clonal reproduction and faces
herbivory from native generalists in its introduced range (Fu
et al. 2024). We tested three hypotheses: (1) herbivory reduces
growth of parental plants while triggering compensatory
responses in leaf traits; (2) PH enhances offspring performance
through both improved survival and growth and increased her-
bivory defence; (3) genotypes differ in their transgenerational
responses, with some lineages showing stronger parental effects
than others. By examining both growth enhancement and
defensive capabilities across genotypes, our study could eluci-
date whether transgenerational plasticity can compensate for
evolutionary trade-offs associated with enemy release.
MATERIALS AND METHODS
Study species
Hydrocotyle verticillata, previously misidentiﬁed as Hydrocotyle
vulgaris L. in China, is a perennial clonal herb native to South
and North America and Southern Africa (Miao et al. 2011;
Wang et al. 2020). Introduced to China as an ornamental plant
in the 1990s, this species has become invasive across tropical
and subtropical regions, spreading predominantly through
clonal growth rather than sexual reproduction (Miao
et al. 2011; Wang et al. 2020; Fu et al. 2024). In its invaded
range, H. verticillata experiences herbivory from native
generalist insects including S. litura, which feed on its leaves
and potentially constrain population expansion (Fu
et al. 2024).
Six genotypes of H. verticillata (designated G1–G6) were col-
lected from geographically distinct ﬁeld populations across
China, with an average distance of 184 km between sampling
sites (Table S1 and Fig. S1). Genotypic identity was conﬁrmed
using ampliﬁed fragment length polymorphism (AFLP) follow-
ing Wang et al. (2020). Since 2016, clonal ramets of each geno-
type have been maintained separately under standardized
greenhouse conditions at Taizhou University.
S. litura (Lepidoptera: Noctuidae) is a polyphagous general-
ist herbivore native to China and now distributed across tropi-
cal and subtropical regions globally (Maes 2022; EPPO 2025).
Its larvae undergo six instars and feed on diverse host plants
including crops and wild species (Zhao et al. 2019). In China,
S. litura has been observed feeding on invasive H. verticillata,
causing substantial defoliation (Fu et al. 2024). Experimental
larvae were obtained from Henan Jiyuan Baiyun Industrial Co.,
Ltd., a commercial supplier specializing in biological control
agents (http://www.keyunnpv.cn/). Prior to experiments, larvae
were reared on a standardized artiﬁcial diet (primarily wheat
germ-based) in individual Petri dishes (10 cm diameter) under
controlled conditions to ensure uniform size and developmen-
tal stage.
The experiment was conducted in a greenhouse at Taizhou
University, Zhejiang Province, China, with mean temperature
of 22.69 °C  0.08 °C and mean photosynthetically active radi-
ation of 357.6 μmol m2 s1 .
Experimental design
Parental generation
The study employed a two-factor factorial design incorporating
six genotypes of H. verticillata and two herbivory treatments
(control versus herbivory by S. litura; Fig. 1A), with 24 repli-
cates per genotype × treatment combination.
On July 4, 2022, 288 stem fragments (48 per genotype) of
H. verticillata were prepared, each consisting of a single-node
stem fragment containing one peltate leaf, intact petiole, and
two 1-cm internodes. This morphological standardization min-
imized size variation among individuals of the same genotype
in the parental generation. Stem fragments were initially rooted
in aquatic containers (52.5 cm long × 34.5 cm wide × 6.5 cm
high) for 3 weeks, using a hydroponic solution of commercial
liquid fertilizer (Scotts Miracle-Gro All Purpose; Scotts
Miracle-Gro Co., Wuhan, China) diluted 1:400 (v/v) in tap
water, refreshed weekly.
On July 27, 2022, rooted fragments were transplanted indi-
vidually into pots (12 cm in diameter × 15.2 cm high), contain-
ing homogenized loess-sand substrate (1:1 v/v), yielding 288
potted plants. Plants were watered to saturation every 2 days
during the 3-week establishment period.
On August 17, 2022, plants were transferred to nylon mesh
cages (180 cm long × 60 cm wide × 60 cm high; 0.5 mm mesh).
Each cage was divided into three compartments (60 cm long
× 60 cm wide × 60 cm high) by transparent partitions
(Fig. 1B). A total of 48 independent compartments were used,
with six different genotypes planted in each compartment.
Treatments (control or herbivory) were randomly assigned to
cages, with all plants within the same cage receiving the same
